The scatter plots (a) and eccentricity histograms (b) demon-
strate the strict spatial separation of the interpolation regime
(inner radius) from the pure extrapolation regime (outer ra-
dius) across both the semantic and syntactic manifolds.
Table 2 reveals a massive drop in absolute performance compared to
the main experiment (e.g., peak pass@1 drops from ∼30% to ∼2%).
This confirms that the presence of multiple syntactic alternatives
in standard datasets acts as a significant crutch for the transformer
model. However, the core relative dynamics hold perfectly: the

(Sem/Syn) test sets confirms their strict out-of-distribution
nature, forcing the models to generalize without the crutch
of semantically identical syntactic alternatives.

Diverse training strategy remains the most robust against out-
of-distribution shifts, maintaining stable (albeit low) performance
across all test splits. In contrast, models trained strictly on a single
manifold (Semantic or Syntactic) suffer severe degradation when
evaluated out-of-distribution.
RQ2: Support generalization. Can the model extrapolate to novel
structures and functional behaviors outside the support of its training
data?
Table 3 strongly reinforces our findings regarding extrapolation.
When evaluating on unseen functional behaviors (semantic extrap-
olation), the model does not suffer a relative drop; it even slightly
improves relative to its interpolation baseline. Conversely, extrapo-
lating to unseen syntactic structures triggers an immense perfor-
mance collapse, with relative metrics dropping by over 55%. This

transitioning from interpolation (within training support)
to pure extrapolation (outside support) evaluated across

Figure 16: Scaling laws for generalization. Performance
(pass@1) as a function of compute (FLOPs). (a–c) Density
generalization: All models exhibit strictly log-linear improve-
ments regardless of the sampling strategy. (d–e) Support gen-
eralization: Semantic extrapolation (d) scales identically to
interpolation, whereas structural extrapolation (e) suffers a
persistent OOD penalty that cannot be easily overcome by
simply scaling up compute.
continues to indicate that when forced to learn a mapping of syn-
tax to semantics, transformers can extrapolate behaviorally using
known structures, but fail to invent novel structural combinations.
RQ3: Scaling behavior. How is generalization performance influ-
enced by model scale?
As shown in Figure 16, scaling compute yields continuous improve-
ments, but the trajectory remains strictly log-linear. Furthermore,
Figure 16e demonstrates that the massive gap between syntactic
interpolation and extrapolation persists across all model scales.
Removing semantic redundancies does not alter the architectural
bottleneck: exponential compute is required for linear gains on
strictly OOD problem instances.

Figure 17: Distance to nearest training neighbor. Comparing
distance of solved (blue) vs. failed (black) test instances across
semantic (top) and syntactic (bottom) manifolds. When eval-
uated out-of-distribution (panels b and c), models success-
fully solve instances that are significantly closer to their
pre-training support, indicating a reliance on local manifold
interpolation.
RQ4: Qualitative analysis. What geometric conditions dictate whether
a model successfully generalizes to a novel instance?
Figure 17 maps the distance to the nearest training neighbor for
solved vs. failed instances. Mirroring the main paper, successful
OOD evaluations (solved cases in panels b and c) are on average
closer to the training support than failed instances. This hints that
also in a strictly deduplicated program space, transformer general-
ization relies on local geometric interpolation from known training
anchors.
RQ5: Test-time scaling. How is generalization performance influ-
enced by test-time scaling, i.e., the number of sampled candidates 𝑘?
Density generalization. Figure 18 tracks density generalization
across different sampling budgets (𝑘∈{1, 5, 10}). While generating
more candidates naturally raises absolute performance across the
board, the underlying log-linear scaling curves and relative model
rankings remain completely rigid. The Diverse model maintains its
robust OOD superiority regardless of how many beams are sampled.
Support generalization. Figure 19 evaluates test-time scaling for
extrapolation. Crucially, the syntactic extrapolation gap (the shaded
area in the bottom row) cannot be closed by simply increasing 𝑘.
Generating more candidate programs helps the model find solutions
within the interpolation regime, but does almost nothing to help it
guess structurally novel AST combinations outside its pre-training
support.
RQ6: Qualitative analysis. What are the characteristics of the gen-
erated programs, and what drives generation failure?

makes the synthesis task vastly more difficult, absolute accu-
racy is drastically reduced compared to the main experiment.
However, the fundamental log-linear scaling dynamics and
the relative robustness of the Diverse training strategy re-
main entirely consistent.

penalty (shaded area, bottom row) remains severe and widens
with increased scale and sampling budgets, confirming that
test-time scaling cannot overcome the model’s limitations to
synthesize unseen syntactic structures.
Length and complexity. Figure 20 illustrates the extreme difficulty
of the semantic uniqueness constraint. A much smaller amount of
problems overall is solved, in tendency shorter programs are solved
to a higher amount than longer ones.

Figure 21: Error analysis of failed generations. Distribution
of the minimum mean squared error (MSE) among gener-
ated candidates. Even on this highly constrained dataset, the
models rarely produce invalid syntax (Syn. Err.). Failures
remain predominantly semantic "near-misses," clustering
at lower error magnitudes (10−3 to 10−6) where the model
approximates the target behavior without achieving perfect
functional equivalence.
Error distributions. Figure 21 confirms that the models still learn
the grammar perfectly; syntactic validity errors (Syn. Err.) remain
negligible. Furthermore, the MSE of failed programs still clusters
at the lower end of the error spectrum (10−3 to 10−6). The Model
frequently generates structurally valid program that are seman-
tically very close to the target function. It often fails to nail the
exact canonical expression required to pass the strict functional
equivalence test.

Interpolation
Extrapolation
(a) Local Approximation
True Function
Model Approximation
Training Data
(b) Global Data Manifold
True Data Manifold
Model Learned Manifold
Training Distribution
Solved Task (ID)
Failed Task (OOD)
Figure 22: Manifold approximation (1D Example). (a) Local
approximation: The model successfully fits a curve (dashed
red) to the training data (blue dots) within the interpola-
tion regime (green). Near the boundary, the approximation
may hold briefly, allowing for "near-OOD" extrapolation.
However, as it moves further into the pure extrapolation
regime (red), the approximation breaks down entirely, di-
verging from the true function (solid gray). (b) Global data
manifold: Looking at it globally, the model only learns the
local curvature of the manifold where data is present. Solved
OOD tasks (green circles) occur slightly outside the training
bounds where the learned trajectory still aligns with the true
manifold, whereas failed tasks (red crosses) occur where the
true manifold diverges into unseen geometry.
5
Discussion: Manifold Approximation
To explain the empirical behaviors observed in our controlled
experiments—specifically the strict log-linear scaling bottlenecks,
the tight correlation between nearest-neighbor proximity and suc-
cess, and the severe penalty of structural extrapolation—we discuss
potential explanations for this behavior. We posit that autoregres-
sive transformers do not inherently learn decomposable building
blocks and assemble them from first principles to solve novel prob-
lems. Instead, they seem to learn a continuous, high-dimensional
approximation of the data manifold defined by their pre-training
distribution. Generalization, in this representation, becomes a mea-
sure of geometric proximity: the model successfully synthesizes
solutions for novel problem instances if and only if they fall within,
or very close to, the local geometry it has explicitly learned to ap-
proximate. This aligns with findings by [2], who demonstrated that
transformers rely on local pattern matching and struggle with true
compositional extrapolation on multi-step reasoning tasks.
Local approximation. As conceptually illustrated in Figure 22a,
we hypothesize that the model fits a local surface to the discrete
training data, akin to a high-dimensional spline or polynomial re-
gression. Within the interpolation regime, defined by the support
of the training data, the model is highly constrained by surround-
ing data anchors, allowing for robust and accurate synthesis. As
a query moves just beyond the boundary of the training data, the
local gradient or curvature learned at the edge of the distribution
may remain valid for a short distance. This explains why the model
occasionally solves extrapolation tasks that possess a very close
nearest neighbor in the training set. However, as the target moves
Interpolation
Zone
Extrapolation Zone
(a) Training Coverage
(b) Manifold Divergence
Training Data
True Data Manifold
Model's Learned Surface
Solved Case (Interpolation)
Failed Case (Extrapolation Gap)
Figure 23: Manifold approximation (2D Example). (a) Train-
ing coverage: A top-down view showing dense training data
in the center. The interpolation zone covers the area where
data density is high, making successful synthesis highly prob-
able (green star). (b) Manifold divergence: A 3D view showing
the model’s learned flat plane (red grid) approximating the
true non-linear data manifold (gray surface) locally. In the
center, the approximation is highly accurate. However, as
one moves into the extrapolation zone, the true manifold
curves upward while the model’s learned approximation con-
tinues linearly, causing a massive divergence that results in
generalization failure (red cross).
further into the pure extrapolation regime, the unconstrained ap-
proximation diverges wildly from the true underlying function,
leading to generalization failure.
Global data manifold. When projecting this concept onto a global
structure (Figure 22b), the limitations of pure neural generation
become apparent. Assuming the target programs exist on a highly
complex, non-linear true data manifold. The model only learns
the specific sub-manifold where it receives a dense learning signal.
When forced to extrapolate to entirely novel behaviors or struc-
tures, the model simply extends its learned local geometry into un-
explored space. If the true data manifold sharply curves or exhibits
novel geometric features which cannot be explained or mapped to
known syntactic structures, the model’s approximation completely
misses the target manifold, resulting in the relatively high absolute
syntactic and semantic error rates, respectively, the low to modest
and log-linearly increasing generalization performance observed
during out-of-distribution evaluation.
Training coverage. Figure 23 illustrates this in another example.
In the center, the true non-linear data manifold (the gray bowl) is
reasonably approximated by the model’s learned flat plane (the red
grid). For test points within this dense interpolation zone (green
star), the approximation gap is minimal, and the model succeeds.
However, in the extrapolation zone, the true manifold curves up-
ward while the model’s approximation continues flatly, creating an
insurmountable divergence gap (red cross). The example illustrates
the importance of a widespread coverage of the data manifold via ap-
propriate training data selection. By diversely sampling data across


--- Page 25 ---
GECCO Companion ’26, July 13–17, 2026, San Jose, Costa Rica
Henrik Voigt, Michael Habeck, and Joachim Giesen
the entire manifold during pre-training, more accurate structural
"anchor points" across the wider curvature of the true manifold can
be learned by the model. This forces the model’s approximation
surface to bend and conform to the true global geometry, minimiz-
ing the divergence gap globally and thereby maximizing out-of-
distribution robustness.
References

preprint arXiv:2107.03374 (2021).

Lin, Sean Welleck, Peter West, Chandra Bhagavatula, Ronan Le Bras, et al. 2023.
Faith and fate: Limits of transformers on compositionality. Advances in neural
information processing systems 36 (2023), 70293–70332.
[3] Haotian Liu, Chunyuan Li, Qingyang Wu, and Yong Jae Lee. 2023. Visual in-
struction tuning. Advances in neural information processing systems 36 (2023),
34892–34916.

Kirpichev, Matthew Rocklin, AMiT Kumar, Sergiu Ivanov, Jason K. Moore, Sar-

Fernando, Sumith Kulal, Robert Cimrman, and Anthony Scopatz. 2017. SymPy:
symbolic computing in Python. PeerJ Computer Science 3 (Jan. 2017), e103.

time compute optimally can be more effective than scaling model parameters.
arXiv preprint arXiv:2408.03314 (2024).

distance between trees and related problems. SIAM J. Comput. 18, 6 (1989), 1245–
